ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to butter cake exists; A00KP (Cakes and pastries) used as the closest defensible parent category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for butter cake as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of butter cake is one slice weighing approximately 80 g, as commonly portioned in Singaporean bakeries and home settings.

nutrient_source.title: Butter Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Butter Cake (cr_id F02010003). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cake and Baked Goods — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 67 (medium) assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for butter cake / plain cake analogues. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (67) × carbohydrate per serving (51.82 g × 0.80 serving fraction = 41.46 g) / 100 = 27.8. This is consistent with the input record's embedded GI value of 67 at medium classification.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (30.16 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.13 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 244 mg/100 g is borderline elevated; saturated fat at ~10.5 g/100 g contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit butter cake due to its high sugar content promoting insulin-driven sodium retention, borderline sodium level, and high saturated fat contributing to vascular stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 30.16 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g threshold; carbohydrate at 51.82 g/100 g with only 1.13 g/100 g dietary fibre produces a severely high glycaemic response; GI of 67 (medium) with high carbohydrate load yields a high glycaemic load per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter cake is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes — the very high sugar content (30.16 g/100 g), high refined carbohydrate load, and minimal fibre will cause significant postprandial blood glucose spikes; this food should be avoided or consumed only in very small occasional portions under medical guidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at approximately 10.5 g/100 g from butter raises LDL cholesterol; trans fat at ~0.1 g/100 g is pro-atherogenic; high sugar (30.16 g/100 g) promotes triglyceride synthesis and systemic inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid butter cake due to its high saturated fat content raising LDL cholesterol, the presence of trans fat, and high sugar promoting hypertriglyceridaemia and vascular inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter cake is a classic baked dessert widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, made from wheat flour, butter, eggs, sugar, and condensed milk. The cake is prepared by creaming butter and sugar together, incorporating eggs and condensed milk, then folding in flour before baking at moderate oven temperatures until golden. The result is a dense, moist, and richly flavoured cake with a tender crumb and a characteristic buttery aroma. In Singapore, butter cake is a staple at bakeries, coffee shops, and home celebrations. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 401 kcal, 6.01 g protein, 19.03 g fat (of which approximately 10.5 g is saturated fat from butter), 51.82 g carbohydrate, and 30.16 g sugar — making it a high-energy, high-sugar food. Dietary fibre is low at 1.13 g/100 g and sodium is 244 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is 67 (medium category), but the high carbohydrate and sugar content produce a high glycaemic load per serving. Clinically, butter cake is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its very high sugar, saturated fat, and moderate sodium content. It should be treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary item.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter cake?
Is butter cake suitable for diabetics?
What is the sugar content of butter cake?
Is butter cake high in fat?
Can I eat butter cake if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of butter cake?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02010003) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI value of 67 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database analogues for butter cake.
notes: saturated_fat_g value of 10.5 g/100 g is estimated from the HPB record context and standard butter cake composition; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat — this is an approximation based on the known fatty acid profile of butter (approximately 55% saturated fat of total fat 19.03 g). trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is an estimate consistent with trace trans fat from butter; the HPB source does not explicitly report trans fat. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB butter cake entry (cr_id F02010003) are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026, and no supplementary data was found in USDA FoodData Central for an exact equivalent: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Glycaemic load per serving calculated using 80 g serving size: GL = 67 × (51.82 × 0.80) / 100 = 27.8.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (401.0), protein_g (6.01), fat_g (19.03), carbohydrate_g (51.82) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F02010003) at confidence 0.95.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.13), sodium_mg (244.0), saturated_fat_g (10.5), sugar_g (30.16) all present as numeric values. saturated_fat_g is an HPB-consistent estimate documented in notes.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (67), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (27.8), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for butter cake; estimation basis documented.

FoodEx2 code A00KP (Cakes and pastries) used as closest_parent defensible anchor with gap note. foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' per allowed values. SNOMED CT omitted honestly with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

Coverage includes English variants (Butter Cake, Butter cake, Butter sponge cake, Pound cake), Chinese (黄油蛋糕, 牛油蛋糕), Malay (Kek mentega), Tamil (வெண்ணெய் கேக்), Hindi (मक्खन केक). Aliases consistency rule verified: all aliases_by_language values appear verbatim in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual local_names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi), populated aliases_by_language, added saturated_fat_g and trans_fat_g, replaced 'diabetes' key with 'type2_diabetes', added cardiovascular_disease condition, populated ontology with FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors, added full llm_training section, documented all -1 sentinel values in notes, calculated glycemic_load_per_serving, set nutrient_source with HPB reference, populated serving section.
Iteration 2: Verified aliases consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array; confirmed all values present verbatim.

Iteration 3: Fixed g3_numeric_hygiene — expanded notes to explicitly list and justify all -1 sentinel fields with source consulted (HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central). Fixed g5_ontology_honesty — corrected foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Singaporean multilingual naming fully satisfied. GI of 67 (medium) from University of Sydney GI Database analogue with documented estimation basis. All three required health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible and honest.
